| $n$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{k/n}\cdot\frac1n$ |
|---:|---:|
| $100$ | $5.19$ |
| $200$ | $5.88$ |
| $300$ | $6.28$ |
| $400$ | $6.57$ |
| $500$ | $6.79$ |

options.A: The limit of the Riemann sums is a finite number less than $10$.
options.B: The limit of the Riemann sums is a finite number greater than $10$.
options.C: The limit of the Riemann sums does not exist because $\frac{1}{n/n}$ does not approach $0$.
options.D: The limit of the Riemann sums does not exist because it is a sum of infinitely many positive numbers.
options.E: The limit of the Riemann sums does not exist because $\int_0^1\frac1x\,dx$ does not exist.

options.B: There is a number $c$ in the open interval $(a,b)$ such that $f(a)<f(c)<f(b)$.
options.C: There is a number $c$ in the closed interval $[a,b]$ such that $f(c)\ge f(x)$ for all $x$ in $[a,b]$.
options.D: There is a number $c$ in the open interval $(a,b)$ such that $f\prime(c)=0$.
options.E: There is a number $c$ in the open interval $(a,b)$ such that $f\prime(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}$.
